current_fact.fact_text: On March 11, 2010, Sebastián Piñera was sworn in as President of Chile while the ground literally shook beneath the ceremony—significant aftershocks from the 2010 Pichilemu earthquakes rippled through central Chile. Politics and tectonics collided in a reminder that power is always built on a restless Earth.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

current_fact.match_reason: The sudden aftershocks during an inauguration feel like a literal "electric shock" running through a nation, echoing the episode’s jolt-of-energy theme.

queue.fact_text: On March 11, 1927, Samuel "Roxy" Rothafel opened the Roxy Theatre in New York City, a movie palace so lavish it was called the "Cathedral of the Motion Picture." With nearly 6,000 seats, it turned film-going into a grand, immersive ritual of light and sound.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

queue.match_reason: The birth of a colossal, immersive theatre aligns with Sedna FM’s own inaugural, big-hearted first episode—both "where it all began" for a new way of experiencing sound.

queue.fact_text: On March 11, 1888, the Great Blizzard of 1888 began ripping along the U.S. East Coast, burying cities like New York in snowdrifts over four meters high. Telegraph lines snapped, trains froze in place, and more than 400 people died in one of North America’s most paralyzing storms.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

queue.match_reason: A blizzard that literally slows a whole region to a halt connects to an episode about enforced slowness and quiet rhythms on a "slow planet."

queue.fact_text: On March 11, 1982, Widerøe Flight 933, a Twin Otter aircraft, crashed into the icy Barents Sea near Gamvik, Norway, killing all 15 on board. The tragedy led to renewed scrutiny of aviation safety in some of the harshest flying conditions on Earth.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: Both the crash and the episode inhabit the same Arctic seascapes of northern Norway, contrasting human fragility with the cold majesty of polar waters.

queue.fact_text: On March 11, 1941, U.S. President Franklin D. Roosevelt signed the Lend-Lease Act, allowing the United States to ship war materials to Allied nations on credit or lease. This quietly transformed the U.S. into the "arsenal of democracy" long before it officially entered World War II.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

queue.match_reason: Lend-Lease was a strategic learning curve in global power—testing new ways of supporting allies—echoing the episode’s theme of experimenting, adjusting, and growing through trial and error.

queue.fact_text: On March 11, 1945, the Imperial Japanese Navy launched Operation Tan No. 2, a large-scale kamikaze mission against the U.S. Pacific Fleet at Ulithi Atoll. Pilots flew one-way attacks in explosive-laden aircraft, turning their own planes into guided weapons.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

queue.match_reason: A desperate, lethal tactic in war ties to an episode about aggressive political resistance and explosive confrontation through music.

queue.fact_text: On March 11, 1851, Giuseppe Verdi’s opera "Rigoletto" premiered in Venice, stunning audiences with its dark storyline and melodies that audiences hummed as they left the theatre. The Duke’s song "La donna è mobile" was such an instant hit that Verdi kept it secret until opening night to prevent street musicians from spoiling the surprise.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

queue.match_reason: An operatic masterpiece’s premiere pairs with an episode tracing another intense, theatrical vocal tradition—Flamenco—as both reinvent the emotional power of the human voice on stage.

queue.fact_text: On March 11, 1845, during the Flagstaff War in New Zealand, Māori chiefs Hōne Heke and Kawiti led warriors who cut down the British flagpole at Kororāreka for the fourth time. The act was a pointed protest against mistranslations and broken promises in the Treaty of Waitangi.

episode.title: Morning Drops #29 - Berber France Meets Arabic Italy
episode.description: Yasmin takes us back to her university years, where she met Abdel - an Algerian-French friend with a big heart and endless optimism. Their friendship grew around music, identity, and playful arguments about whether a song was originally Italian or French. Music that exists in both versions, alongside stories of finding a home, boxing training, and car rides filled with music.

queue.match_reason: A conflict born from mistranslation and contested sovereignty mirrors an episode about shifting identities, languages, and belonging between cultures.

queue.fact_text: On March 11, 2018, a Bombardier Challenger 604 business jet crashed into Iran’s Zagros Mountains en route from Sharjah to Istanbul, killing all 11 people aboard. The aircraft’s flight data indicated it had climbed steeply and lost control amid rapidly changing weather over the rugged terrain.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

queue.match_reason: A tragic journey cut short through mountains sits against an episode about travel as moving meditation, highlighting the vulnerability and meaning we invest in movement across landscapes.

queue.fact_text: On March 11, 1981, protests by students at the University of Pristina in Kosovo erupted over living conditions and political status, quickly spreading into mass demonstrations across the province. The unrest became a catalyst in the long, complex struggle over Kosovo’s autonomy within Yugoslavia.

episode.title: Morning Drops #08 - (un)Boxing
episode.description: An episode about breaking free from expectations and labels. Music that helps us unbox ourselves and embrace who we truly are.
songs: Kendrick Lamar - The Blacker the Berry

queue.match_reason: Student protests demanding more rights resonate with an episode themed around breaking boxes, labels, and imposed identities.

queue.fact_text: On March 11, 1387, the Battle of Castagnaro saw the forces of Padua, commanded by English mercenary John Hawkwood, defeat the army of Verona. Hawkwood used a feigned retreat and carefully chosen terrain to outmaneuver a larger foe, turning tactics into art.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

queue.match_reason: A medieval Italian battlefield in the Veneto region connects to an Italian, landscape-infused episode about mountains and strategic terrain, both shaped by geography and storytelling.

queue.fact_text: On March 11, 1343 (Old Style), Arnošt of Pardubice became the last Bishop of Prague; a year later he would be elevated as the city’s first Archbishop. His promotion marked Prague’s rising importance in Central Europe and reshaped the region’s religious hierarchy.

episode.title: Morning Drops #28 - Under Scottish Skies
episode.description: Gaia shares a personal moment of transition — a shift in seasons, in rhythm, and in life. From this space of quiet change comes a musical journey through Scotland: not the usual names, but voices that speak from the edges. A personal ritual and a small act of curiosity: an invitation to notice what grounds us when everything moves.
songs: Paolo Nutini - Pencil Full of Lead

queue.match_reason: A shift from bishop to archbishop is a structural transition in spiritual leadership, echoing an episode about life transitions and the quiet rituals that ground us under changing skies.

queue.fact_text: On March 11, 2023, soldiers of Myanmar’s military carried out the Pinlaung massacre in Shan State, killing at least 30 villagers including three Buddhist monks taking shelter in a monastery. The atrocity became one of the stark symbols of escalating violence after the 2021 coup.

episode.title: Morning Drops #35 - Thailand
episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
songs: Burna Boy - Cheat on Me (feat. Dave)

queue.match_reason: Geographically close and culturally linked, the Thai-focused episode offers a softer, human-scale lens on mainland Southeast Asia set against Myanmar’s stark violence.

queue.fact_text: On March 11, 2020, the World Health Organization officially declared COVID‑19 a pandemic, acknowledging that the new coronavirus had spread globally. That single announcement crystallized a shift in everyday life, from travel and work to how humans relate to distance and touch.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

queue.match_reason: A global pandemic that pushed life online connects directly to an episode examining our intense relationship with digital devices and mediated connection.

queue.fact_text: On March 11, 2006, Michelle Bachelet was inaugurated as Chile’s first female president, a former political exile and torture survivor stepping into the country’s highest office. Her election symbolized both democratic recovery and a shift toward greater gender representation in Latin American politics.

episode.title: Morning Drops #04 - Bars With Balls
episode.description: Yasmin dives into Women's Day through the voices of four incredible female rappers. Morning fire from the magic planet of Sedna FM — move your hands up and down with us. Hip hop culture through a feminine lens.
songs: Little Simz - Point and Kill (feat. Obongjayar)

queue.match_reason: The rise of Chile’s first female president pairs with an episode celebrating powerful female voices in hip-hop, both amplifying women taking space in traditionally male arenas.

queue.fact_text: On March 11, 1985, Mikhail Gorbachev became General Secretary of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union, effectively the USSR’s last head of state. His policies of glasnost and perestroika opened political and economic debates that would ultimately help dissolve the superpower he led.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

queue.match_reason: Gorbachev’s internal revolution inside the Soviet system links with an episode about Residente, whose politically charged art also tries to dismantle giants from within.

queue.fact_text: On March 11, 1977, a group of Hanafi Muslims seized three buildings in Washington, D.C., taking around 150 hostages in a days-long siege. Diplomats from Egypt, Pakistan, and Iran helped negotiate the peaceful release of the captives, averting further bloodshed.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

queue.match_reason: An armed action framed in Islamic terms and resolved through diplomacy connects with an episode rooted in medina atmospheres and Arabic-inflected soundscapes, reflecting the cultural backdrop in a peaceful, sonic way.

queue.fact_text: Also on March 11, 2008’s STS‑123 mission, astronauts carried the Canadian-built Dextre robot to the ISS, a two-armed "handyman" designed to perform delicate maintenance outside the station. It was a step toward more autonomous, robotic caretaking of human habitats in orbit.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

queue.match_reason: A new robotic "helper" joining the ISS mirrors the launch of a new show format; both quietly expand what’s possible in their orbit, with no fixed rules except exploration.

queue.fact_text: The 2011 Tōhoku earthquake was powerful enough that it shifted Earth’s axis by several centimeters and shortened the length of the day by microseconds. Our planet’s rotation literally changed in a matter of minutes as tectonic plates lurched past each other deep under the ocean.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

queue.match_reason: A quake that subtly alters Earth’s rhythm parallels a genre like trip‑hop that subtly changes the rhythmic feel of music, bending time with downtempo beats and underwater atmospheres.

queue.fact_text: The Fukushima Daiichi nuclear accident that began on March 11, 2011, involved three reactor meltdowns after tsunami waves overwhelmed seawalls and backup generators. It forced over 100,000 people to evacuate and sparked a global rethinking of nuclear safety and energy policy.

episode.title: Morning Drops #47 - The Rise Of Autotune
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the explosive rise of Auto-Tune, a tool that quietly changed the sound of music without asking for attention. From fragile, imperfect recordings to voices shaped with precision, this episode follows the moment when correction became standard and sound became controlled.

Along the way, we discover the curious story of how it was invented, how it moved from the background of studios to the center of debate, and how a few artists turned it into something expressive rather than hidden.

Listen slowly, and let us know what you think about Autotune.
songs: Chet Baker – I Fall in Love Too Easily

queue.match_reason: A high-tech system pushed to failure and global debate about its safety mirrors the story of Auto‑Tune: a powerful technology that quietly became standard, then controversial, changing how we think about control and risk.

queue.fact_text: The 2010 Pichilemu earthquakes that rattled Chile during Piñera’s inauguration were intraplate quakes, generated within a tectonic plate rather than at a plate boundary. They revealed that even regions already devastated by a major subduction quake could still hide their own, more local seismic surprises.

episode.title: Morning Drops #48 - Cosmic Monday
episode.description:
Cosmic Monday is a Morning Drops episode built around four Italian dance records, tracing how disco rhythms slowly became electronic language.
Gaia moves through four tracks like chapters: groove first, then machines, then that bright, futuristic feeling you only get when the bassline is doing the talking. It's not nostalgia, exactly. It's more like returning to a sound that still works — especially on mornings when you need a little extra energy, but you don't want anything aggressive.
Take it as a slow transmission. Let it carry you into the day.
songs: Giovanotti Mondani Meccanici - Don't Ask Me Why

queue.match_reason: Hidden quakes within a plate changing the Earth’s internal rhythm connect to an episode about evolving dance rhythms—groove shifting from organic to machine-driven, yet still quietly shaking the floor.

published.fact_text: On March 11, 2008, Space Shuttle Endeavour launched mission STS-123, delivering Japan’s first Kibō module to the International Space Station. It marked Japan’s largest contribution to human spaceflight—a laboratory whose name means “hope,” orbiting Earth at 28,000 km/h.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A spaceflight mission named for hope fits a mindset "space shift" episode that reimagines emptiness as creative space.

published.fact_text: On March 11, 2011, a magnitude 9.0 megathrust earthquake struck off the coast of Sendai, Japan, unleashing a tsunami that devastated entire towns. The disaster triggered the Fukushima nuclear accident, one of only two events rated Level 7 on the International Nuclear Event Scale.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: An episode about storms, resilience, and transition mirrors the earthquake–tsunami–nuclear cascade and the long recovery that followed.

published.fact_text: On March 11, 1641, Guaraní fighters living in Jesuit reductions defeated Portuguese-allied bandeirantes at the Battle of Mbororé. Their victory slowed the slave-raiding expeditions that had been tearing Indigenous communities apart in the region.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

published.match_reason: Indigenous Guaraní resistance to colonial violence pairs with Tuareg music born of exile, memory, and survival under external pressures.

published.fact_text: On March 11, 2010, Sebastián Piñera was sworn in as President of Chile while the ground literally shook beneath the ceremony—significant aftershocks from the 2010 Pichilemu earthquakes rippled through central Chile. Politics and tectonics collided in a reminder that power is always built on a restless Earth.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: The sudden aftershocks during an inauguration feel like a literal "electric shock" running through a nation, echoing the episode’s jolt-of-energy theme.
